name: Health & Medicine
description: Test your knowledge of the human body and medicine.

questions.text: How many bones are in an adult human body?
questions.explanation: The adult human body has 206 bones, though babies are born with around 270 bones that fuse together over time.

questions.text: What organ pumps blood throughout the body?
questions.explanation: The heart is a muscular organ that pumps blood through the circulatory system, supplying oxygen and nutrients to the body.

questions.text: Which vitamin is produced by the skin when exposed to sunlight?
questions.explanation: The skin produces Vitamin D when exposed to ultraviolet B (UVB) radiation from sunlight, essential for bone health.

questions.text: What is the largest organ of the human body?
questions.explanation: The skin is the largest organ of the human body, covering an average area of about 2 square meters in adults.

questions.text: What does CPR stand for?
questions.explanation: CPR stands for Cardiopulmonary Resuscitation, an emergency procedure combining chest compressions and rescue breathing.

questions.text: Which blood type is known as the universal donor?
questions.explanation: O negative blood type is called the universal donor because it can be transfused to patients of any blood type in emergencies.

questions.text: What is the normal resting heart rate range for adults?
questions.explanation: A normal resting heart rate for adults is 60-100 beats per minute, though trained athletes may have lower rates.

questions.text: Which organ filters toxins from the blood?
questions.explanation: The liver is the primary organ responsible for filtering toxins from the blood and processing nutrients from food.

questions.text: How many chambers does the human heart have?
questions.explanation: The human heart has 4 chambers: two upper atria and two lower ventricles, working together to circulate blood.

questions.text: What is the most common type of blood type in humans?
questions.explanation: Blood type O is the most common blood type worldwide, with O positive being particularly prevalent.

questions.text: Which mineral is essential for strong bones and teeth?
questions.explanation: Calcium is the most abundant mineral in the body and is essential for building and maintaining strong bones and teeth.

questions.text: What is the recommended daily water intake for adults?
questions.explanation: General recommendations suggest about 2-3 liters (8-12 cups) of water per day for adults, varying by activity and climate.

questions.text: Where does digestion begin?
questions.explanation: Digestion begins in the mouth, where saliva contains enzymes (like amylase) that start breaking down food.

questions.text: What does DNA stand for?
questions.explanation: DNA stands for Deoxyribonucleic Acid, the molecule that carries genetic information in all living organisms.

questions.text: Which vitamin is important for vision, especially in low light?
questions.explanation: Vitamin A is essential for the production of rhodopsin, a pigment in the eye needed for vision in low light conditions.

questions.text: How many teeth does a healthy adult have, including wisdom teeth?
questions.explanation: A healthy adult has 32 teeth including 4 wisdom teeth (third molars), though many people have their wisdom teeth removed.

questions.text: What is the function of white blood cells?
questions.explanation: White blood cells (leukocytes) are a key part of the immune system and help the body fight infections and disease.

questions.text: What is the medical term for high blood pressure?
questions.explanation: Hypertension is the medical term for high blood pressure, defined as consistently having blood pressure above 130/80 mmHg.

questions.text: Which part of the brain controls balance and coordination?
questions.explanation: The cerebellum, located at the back of the brain, coordinates voluntary movements, balance, and posture.

questions.text: What type of joint allows the widest range of movement?
questions.explanation: Ball-and-socket joints, like the shoulder and hip, allow movement in multiple directions and have the greatest range of motion.

questions.text: What is the name of the enzyme in saliva that begins carbohydrate digestion?
questions.explanation: Salivary amylase (ptyalin) is the enzyme in saliva that begins breaking down carbohydrates (starches) in the mouth.

questions.text: What is the term for the scientific study of the heart?
questions.explanation: Cardiology is the branch of medicine that deals with the study, diagnosis, and treatment of disorders of the heart.

questions.text: Which hormone regulates blood sugar levels?
questions.explanation: Insulin, produced by the pancreas, regulates blood glucose levels by facilitating the uptake of glucose into cells.

questions.text: Who is credited with developing the first successful polio vaccine?
questions.explanation: Jonas Salk developed the first safe and effective polio vaccine, announced in 1955, which dramatically reduced polio cases worldwide.

questions.text: What is the function of the kidneys?
questions.explanation: The kidneys filter blood to remove waste products and excess fluids, producing urine that is excreted from the body.

questions.text: What is the medical term for the collarbone?
questions.explanation: The clavicle is the medical term for the collarbone, a long bone that connects the sternum to the shoulder blade.

questions.text: Which vitamin deficiency causes scurvy?
questions.explanation: Scurvy is caused by a deficiency of Vitamin C (ascorbic acid), which is essential for collagen synthesis.

questions.text: What is the name of the largest artery in the human body?
questions.explanation: The aorta is the largest artery in the body, carrying oxygenated blood from the heart to distribute throughout the body.

questions.text: How long is the average adult small intestine?
questions.explanation: The small intestine is approximately 6 meters (20 feet) long in adults and is the primary site for nutrient absorption.

questions.text: What is the Heimlich maneuver used for?
options: Dislodging a foreign object from a choking person's airway
questions.explanation: The Heimlich maneuver uses abdominal thrusts to create pressure to dislodge a foreign object blocking the airway of a choking person.

questions.text: Which antibiotic, the first to be widely used, was discovered by Alexander Fleming?
questions.explanation: Alexander Fleming discovered penicillin in 1928 when he noticed that mold (Penicillium) was killing bacteria in a petri dish.

questions.text: What does BMI stand for?
questions.explanation: BMI stands for Body Mass Index, a calculation using height and weight to estimate whether a person has a healthy body weight.

questions.text: What is the medical term for a broken bone?
questions.explanation: A fracture is the medical term for a broken bone, ranging from hairline cracks to complete breaks.

questions.text: Which part of the eye controls the amount of light entering?
questions.explanation: The pupil is the opening in the iris that controls the amount of light entering the eye, dilating in dim light and constricting in bright light.

questions.text: What is the name of the condition where the body's immune system attacks itself?
questions.explanation: Autoimmune diseases occur when the immune system mistakenly attacks the body's own healthy cells and tissues.

questions.text: What is the blood-brain barrier?
options: A surgical procedure to stop brain bleeding
A selective barrier preventing most substances from entering the brain from blood
A network of arteries supplying the brain
questions.explanation: The blood-brain barrier is a highly selective semipermeable border of endothelial cells that regulates what can pass from blood to the brain.

questions.text: What is the medical term for the condition where plaque builds up in the arteries?
questions.explanation: Atherosclerosis is the buildup of fats, cholesterol, and other substances (plaque) in the artery walls, narrowing them and restricting blood flow.

questions.text: Which neurotransmitter is primarily associated with reward and pleasure?
questions.explanation: Dopamine is the primary neurotransmitter associated with the brain's reward and pleasure centers, also involved in movement and motivation.

questions.text: What is the glomerular filtration rate (GFR) used to measure?
questions.explanation: GFR measures how well the kidneys filter waste from the blood; a GFR below 60 mL/min/1.73m² indicates chronic kidney disease.

questions.text: What is the role of telomeres?
questions.explanation: Telomeres are repetitive DNA sequences at chromosome ends that protect them from degradation; they shorten with each cell division and are linked to aging.

questions.text: Which immunoglobulin class is primarily responsible for the secondary immune response?
questions.explanation: IgG is the most abundant antibody in blood and the main antibody in the secondary (memory) immune response, providing long-term immunity.

questions.text: What is the sodium-potassium pump responsible for?
options: Maintaining the electrochemical gradient across cell membranes
questions.explanation: The sodium-potassium (Na+/K+) pump maintains electrochemical gradients by moving 3 Na+ ions out and 2 K+ ions in per ATP consumed.

questions.text: What is the term for programmed cell death?
questions.explanation: Apoptosis is a form of programmed cell death that occurs in multicellular organisms as a normal and controlled part of development and homeostasis.

questions.text: Which part of the nephron is responsible for most reabsorption?
questions.explanation: The proximal convoluted tubule reabsorbs approximately 65% of filtered sodium, water, and most other solutes from the glomerular filtrate.

questions.text: What is the mechanism of action of beta-blocker medications?
options: Block calcium channels in the heart
Block beta-adrenergic receptors, slowing heart rate
questions.explanation: Beta-blockers block beta-adrenergic receptors, reducing the effects of adrenaline, which slows heart rate and lowers blood pressure.

questions.text: What is the difference between a T cell and a B cell?
options: T cells are produced in bone marrow; B cells in the thymus
T cells mature in the thymus and mediate cellular immunity; B cells produce antibodies
T cells fight viruses; B cells fight bacteria only
T cells are larger; B cells are smaller
questions.explanation: T cells mature in the thymus and are responsible for cell-mediated immunity; B cells mature in bone marrow and produce antibodies (humoral immunity).

questions.text: What is the medical term for abnormally low blood sodium levels?
questions.explanation: Hyponatremia is a condition where blood sodium concentration falls below 135 mEq/L, causing symptoms ranging from nausea to seizures.

questions.text: Which enzyme is deficient in people with phenylketonuria (PKU)?
questions.explanation: PKU is caused by a deficiency of phenylalanine hydroxylase, which converts phenylalanine to tyrosine, leading to toxic phenylalanine buildup.

questions.text: What is the Frank-Starling law of the heart?
options: Heart rate increases with body temperature
Stroke volume increases with greater ventricular filling (preload)
Blood pressure equals cardiac output times resistance
Heart muscle contracts faster under sympathetic stimulation
questions.explanation: The Frank-Starling law states that the heart's stroke volume increases in response to an increase in the volume of blood filling the ventricle (preload).

questions.text: What is the significance of the HLA (Human Leukocyte Antigen) system?
options: It encodes proteins critical for immune recognition and organ transplant compatibility
It regulates red blood cell production
questions.explanation: The HLA system encodes cell-surface proteins that allow the immune system to distinguish self from non-self, critical for organ transplant compatibility.
